meta.title: Schematica — Safer configuration for research and engineering
meta.description: Schematica is a schema-aware desktop editor and CLI for safer YAML, JSON, and TOML configuration workflows.
meta.ogTitle: Schematica — Config files, made difficult to get wrong
meta.ogDescription: Edit with schema-generated controls, validate before a run, and compare experiment configurations semantically.
hero.title: Config files, made difficult to get wrong.
hero.lede: Schematica turns YAML, JSON, and TOML into a guided editing workflow—so researchers and engineers can catch configuration mistakes before an experiment, deployment, or batch job begins.
hero.imageAction: Open the schema-driven editor screenshot at full size
hero.imageAlt: Schematica editing a SLURM training configuration with a schema-generated batch-size slider set to 128 samples, a logarithmic learning-rate control, and syntax-highlighted YAML side by side.
hero.captionBody: Schema-generated controls and the source file stay in sync.
workflow.title: From “which config ran?” to a workflow you can trust.
workflow.intro: Configuration is part of the experiment. Schematica makes its constraints, differences, and final values visible without taking the file away from you.
workflow.openBody: Open local files or browse a remote host over SSH. Keep multiple run profiles in one workspace.
workflow.editBody: Enums, ranges, required fields, units, defaults, and descriptions become clear controls.
workflow.compareBody: Compare values by configuration path, independent of whitespace, key order, or file format.
workflow.validateBody: Use the same schema semantics in the desktop app, terminal, scripts, and CI.
compare.title: See the change that changes the result.
compare.body: A line diff makes structured data noisy. Schematica compares parsed values by path, then separates changed, missing, and identical settings.
compare.pointFilter: Filter to differences or inspect every value
compare.pointCli: Use the same comparison engine from the CLI
compare.imageAction: Open the semantic comparison screenshot at full size
compare.imageAlt: Schematica comparing baseline.yaml and slurm.yaml, showing two changed values and one missing value in a semantic table.
schemas.title: Bring a schema. Build one. Or start without one.
schemas.withTitle: Turn constraints into a usable form.
schemas.withBody: JSON Schema drives validation and controls. Optional x-ui hints add units, ordering, notes, and scale without making the schema proprietary.
schemas.withoutTitle: Keep editing, then add structure when ready.
schemas.withoutBody: Schematica can infer a practical form for ordinary documents, preserve access to the raw editor, and let you attach or design a schema later.
schemas.imageAction: Open the schema designer screenshot at full size
schemas.imageAlt: Schematica's visual JSON Schema designer showing schema coverage, field keys, types, titles, and required-field controls.
cli.eyebrow: The same model in your terminal
cli.title: Move from inspection to automation.
cli.body: The desktop app and CLI share the same parser, validator, path model, and semantic diff. What a person reviews can also become a repeatable check in CI.
capabilities.title: A focused workbench, not another platform.
capabilities.sourceBody: Use the form and syntax-highlighted source side by side, with a split you can resize.
capabilities.remoteBody: Discover SSH hosts, browse directories, authenticate when needed, and edit remote configs.
capabilities.validationBody: Issues point back to the affected field and path instead of becoming a detached error log.
capabilities.formatsBody: Work with YAML, JSON, and TOML rather than moving configuration into a hosted database.
capabilities.keyboardBody: Use a command palette, tab navigation, resizable panes, and accessible native controls.
capabilities.platformBody: A lightweight Tauri desktop app for macOS, Windows, and Linux, plus a scriptable CLI.
cta.title: Spend compute on the experiment, not the typo.
cta.body: Schematica is preparing its first public beta. Follow the repository for release builds and progress.
